summary: DeVault argues that privacy should be treated as a hobby and a fundamental right that technically competent people must actively exercise. He dismantles the 'nothing to hide' argument by listing mundane but embarrassing information anyone might want kept private, from financial mismanagement to personal preferences. He then provides a practical guide to reclaiming privacy: deleting Facebook and Google, using Linux, blocking trackers, encrypting drives, self-hosting email, and running open-source firmware. The core message is that giving data to corporations is effectively waiving your Fifth Amendment rights, and the government must never succeed in undermining encryption.
key_points: The 'nothing to hide' argument fails because everyone has legal but embarrassing information they'd want kept private, and today's legal behavior could become tomorrow's liability
No privacy model that includes Facebook or Google actually works — you must eliminate dependence on corporate data collectors before encryption matters
Understanding your own security systems and their weak points is more important than any single tool or technique
Encryption is a right to use math, and government attempts to subvert it must be resisted unconditionally
Self-hosting services like email is painful but essential for true control over personal information
money_quotes: Privacy is my hobby, and should be a hobby of every technically competent American.
Building secure facilities for housing secrets is fun! That's true even if there aren't a lot of interesting secrets to hide there.
To the government: stay the fuck out of my right to encrypt, or, as I like to think of it, my right to use math.
They will try, again and again, to take it from us. They must never win.
No amount of encryption will help you if you give Google a GPS map of your every move and your search history and information about basically every page on the internet you visit.
When you give all of this information to Google, Facebook, and others, you're basically waiving your fifth amendment rights.
A valid privacy & security model does not include Windows.
There's no privacy model that includes Facebook and works. Give up.
key_insight: Privacy must be actively practiced as a skill and habit — not passively hoped for — because the right to encrypt is under constant threat and corporate data collection renders other protections meaningless.
